content.text: assigns one or more labels from a fixed set to a piece of text. It is the most common NLP task in production systems: spam filters, sentiment dashboards, content moderation, topic routing, and support-ticket triage all rely on it. The input is a string of arbitrary length; the output is one or more discrete labels.

attrs.alt: Text classification pipeline: raw text → preprocess → vectorise → classifier → prediction label, with four classification variants shown below

content.text: — exactly two classes: spam/not-spam, positive/negative. Use a single sigmoid output neuron.
— K mutually exclusive classes: news topic (sports/politics/tech/finance). Use softmax output; exactly one class is assigned.
— a document can belong to multiple classes simultaneously: a Stack Overflow question tagged [python, pandas, performance]. Use independent sigmoid per class.
— classes have order: 1–5 star ratings. Can be treated as multiclass or regressed to a continuous score.
For many production use cases, a TF-IDF vectoriser with logistic regression reaches within a few percentage points of much heavier models and trains in seconds. Always start here as a baseline.

from sklearn.datasets import fetch_20newsgroups
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

# Load 4 newsgroup categories
categories = ["sci.space", "rec.sport.hockey", "comp.graphics", "talk.politics.misc"]
train = fetch_20newsgroups(subset="train", categories=categories, remove=("headers", "footers", "quotes"))
test  = fetch_20newsgroups(subset="test",  categories=categories, remove=("headers", "footers", "quotes"))

pipe = Pipeline([
    ("tfidf", TfidfVectorizer(ngram_range=(1, 2), max_features=50_000, sublinear_tf=True)),
    ("clf",  LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, C=5)),
])
pipe.fit(train.data, train.target)

print(classification_report(test.target, pipe.predict(test.data), target_names=categories))
#               precision    recall  f1-score   support
# sci.space          0.95      0.93      0.94       394
# rec.sport.hockey   0.98      0.98      0.98       399
# comp.graphics      0.92      0.94      0.93       389
# talk.politics.misc 0.89      0.88      0.89       310
#
# accuracy                               0.94      1492

When the task involves subtlety — negation, irony, domain-specific jargon, or short texts where word order matters — a pre-trained transformer fine-tuned on labelled examples outperforms TF-IDF. DistilBERT is 40% smaller than BERT with 97% of its accuracy, making it a practical choice.

from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer,
    AutoModelForSequenceClassification,
    TrainingArguments,
    Trainer,
)
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

# SST-2: binary sentiment (positive / negative movie reviews)
dataset = load_dataset("sst2")
model_name = "distilbert-base-uncased"

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)

def tokenize(batch):
    return tokenizer(batch["sentence"], truncation=True, padding="max_length", max_length=128)

tokenized = dataset.map(tokenize, batched=True)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(model_name, num_labels=2)

def compute_metrics(eval_pred):
    logits, labels = eval_pred
    preds = np.argmax(logits, axis=-1)
    return {"accuracy": accuracy_score(labels, preds)}

training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./distilbert-sst2",
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=32,
    per_device_eval_batch_size=64,
    evaluation_strategy="epoch",
    save_strategy="epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    weight_decay=0.01,
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=tokenized["train"],
    eval_dataset=tokenized["validation"],
    compute_metrics=compute_metrics,
)

trainer.train()
# Epoch 3 val accuracy: ~0.927 on SST-2
When you have no labelled data, Hugging Face's zero-shot pipeline uses an NLI (Natural Language Inference) model to score whether a text "entails" each candidate label as a hypothesis.

from transformers import pipeline

classifier = pipeline("zero-shot-classification", model="facebook/bart-large-mnli")

result = classifier(
    "The Fed raised interest rates by 25 basis points to combat inflation.",
    candidate_labels=["finance", "sports", "technology", "politics"],
)
print(result["labels"])  # ['finance', 'politics', 'technology', 'sports']
print(result["scores"])  # [0.76, 0.17, 0.05, 0.02]

# Multi-label: set multi_label=True to allow overlapping classes
result_ml = classifier(
    "This Python tutorial covers pandas and data visualisation.",
    candidate_labels=["python", "data science", "tutorial", "finance"],
    multi_label=True,
)
print(result_ml["labels"])  # ['python', 'tutorial', 'data science', 'finance']
print(result_ml["scores"])  # [0.98, 0.96, 0.91, 0.02]
Misleading on imbalanced data (99% negative → 99% accuracy trivially)
Treats rare classes equally — may over-weight noisy small classes
Can be optimistic on very imbalanced data — prefer PR-AUC
Few-shot with a large LLM or TF-IDF + LR baseline
TF-IDF + Logistic Regression — hard to beat at this scale
TF-IDF + LR — inspect feature weights directly

Always run the TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline first. On many real-world datasets with enough data and reasonable feature engineering, it matches or outperforms fine-tuned transformers while training in seconds instead of hours. A 1% accuracy gain from BERT rarely justifies 100× the compute.

Every high-volume text pipeline eventually needs routing: spam detection, priority triage, moderation, language detection, topic assignment. Without classification, human reviewers become the bottleneck. A spam filter that misses 1% of spam on 10 million emails per day still passes 100,000 spam messages to inboxes. A content moderation system without classification cannot scale to social platforms processing millions of posts per minute.

The economic case is direct. A support team routing tickets by hand to the correct department adds seconds per ticket. With classification, routing is milliseconds and headcount grows sub-linearly with ticket volume. Shopify and Airbnb use text classification to route support tickets, reducing mean response time by 30–50% on high-volume queues. Stripe uses classification to flag fraud notes and legal documents for immediate escalation.
A trained model classifies millions of documents per day on commodity hardware. Human reviewers cannot scale this way.
A model applies the same decision rule across every input, unlike human reviewers whose labeling standards drift over time and across shifts.
TF-IDF + logistic regression classifies a document in under 1 ms. DistilBERT typically takes 5–20 ms per batch item on CPU.
A TF-IDF + logistic regression model exposes its feature weights directly — you can inspect which n-grams drove a spam decision.
Fine-tuning a pre-trained transformer requires only hundreds to thousands of labeled examples, not millions, making high-quality classifiers accessible even for niche domains.

Supervised classification requires labeled examples. Quality labeling is expensive — professional annotators charge $0.10–$1.00 per label depending on difficulty. Budget 200–2,000 labeled examples per class for a fine-tuned transformer.
When product definitions of classes change (e.g., "spam" now includes promotional emails that were previously allowed), labeled data becomes stale and models degrade silently.

Real-world text classification is frequently 1:100 or worse (fraud:legit). Models trained on imbalanced data learn to ignore the minority class. Resampling, class weights, or threshold tuning are required.
BERT-family models accept at most 512 tokens. Long documents — legal briefs, technical reports — must be chunked, and chunk-level predictions aggregated.
Fine-tuned BERT is 40–100× slower than TF-IDF + LR for inference. For high-throughput real-time classification, transformer serving requires GPU or careful quantisation.
Real-World Example: Multi-Topic Support Ticket Router

An e-commerce company receives 50,000 support tickets per day across five departments: billing, shipping, returns, technical, and account. Manual routing added an average of 4 hours to first response time. The following pipeline replaced manual triage, classifying tickets in under 5 ms with 94% accuracy:

import re
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report
import numpy as np

# ---- Synthetic ticket data ----
TICKETS = [
    ("I was charged twice for my order #123", "billing"),
    ("My refund hasn't appeared after 10 days", "billing"),
    ("Package arrived damaged and I need a return label", "returns"),
    ("I want to return the shoes, they don't fit", "returns"),
    ("My order shows delivered but I never received it", "shipping"),
    ("Tracking says in transit for 2 weeks", "shipping"),
    ("The app crashes when I try to log in", "technical"),
    ("Can't reset my password, the link is broken", "account"),
    ("Please change my email address", "account"),
    ("The checkout button doesn't work on Firefox", "technical"),
] * 80  # repeat for demo; real data would be much larger

texts  = [t for t, _ in TICKETS]
labels = [l for _, l in TICKETS]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    texts, labels, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=labels
)

pipe = Pipeline([
    ("tfidf", TfidfVectorizer(
        ngram_range=(1, 2),
        max_features=20_000,
        sublinear_tf=True,
        min_df=2,
        strip_accents="unicode",
    )),
    ("clf", LogisticRegression(
        max_iter=1000,
        C=3.0,
        class_weight="balanced",  # handles class imbalance
    )),
])

pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)
preds = pipe.predict(X_test)
print(classification_report(y_test, preds))

# --- Inference with confidence threshold ---
DEPARTMENTS = {
    "billing":   "billing@company.com",
    "returns":   "returns@company.com",
    "shipping":  "logistics@company.com",
    "technical": "techsupport@company.com",
    "account":   "account@company.com",
}

def route_ticket(text: str, confidence_threshold: float = 0.6) -> dict:
    proba = pipe.predict_proba([text])[0]
    best_idx = np.argmax(proba)
    best_class = pipe.classes_[best_idx]
    best_conf  = proba[best_idx]
    if best_conf < confidence_threshold:
        return {"department": "manual_review", "email": "triage@company.com",
                "confidence": best_conf, "auto_routed": False}
    return {"department": best_class, "email": DEPARTMENTS[best_class],
            "confidence": round(float(best_conf), 3), "auto_routed": True}

samples = [
    "I need to return my jacket — it's the wrong size",
    "Why am I seeing two charges on my credit card statement?",
    "This is not what I expected from your company",  # ambiguous — low confidence
]
for t in samples:
    print(route_ticket(t))
Using accuracy as the only metric on imbalanced data:
A spam filter dataset with 98% not-spam achieves 98% accuracy by always predicting not-spam. Always report F1 per class and confusion matrix.
Teams jump straight to fine-tuning BERT and then discover the TF-IDF baseline would have met requirements at 1/100th the compute. Always establish the classical baseline first.

Fitting the TF-IDF vocabulary on the full dataset (train + test) before splitting creates data leakage — the vocabulary is informed by test-set word frequencies. Fit the vectoriser only on training data.
Using softmax + argmax on a multi-label problem forces a single class prediction. Multi-label requires independent sigmoid thresholds per class with careful per-class calibration.
Not evaluating on a temporally split test set:

For classification of time-stamped text (news, support tickets), a random train/test split inflates accuracy because future data leaks into training. Split on time: train on earlier data, test on later.
Start with TF-IDF + LR, validate quality, then upgrade if needed:
Most classification tasks do not require BERT. The baseline often reaches 90%+ accuracy and trains in under a minute.
Term frequency saturation (1 + log(tf) instead of raw tf) prevents highly repetitive documents from dominating the feature space.

Use CalibratedClassifierCV for scikit-learn models; check that predicted probabilities are well-calibrated (Platt scaling or isotonic regression). A model that predicts 90% confidence should be correct 90% of the time.
Set a confidence threshold and route low-confidence predictions to humans:
An automated classifier that says "I'm 55% sure this is spam" should escalate to a human. 80% confidence threshold is a common starting point.
Track the distribution of predicted classes and confidence scores daily. A sudden increase in low-confidence predictions signals distribution shift or a new input pattern the model hasn't seen.

TF-IDF + logistic regression can classify 100,000–500,000 short texts per second on a single CPU core, making it suitable for real-time inference at any reasonable scale. Fine-tuned DistilBERT on CPU handles roughly 20–100 texts per second (depending on text length and batch size); on a single GPU (e.g., A10G), this rises to 500–2,000 texts per second with batching.
TfidfVectorizer, LogisticRegression, SGDClassifier, and MultinomialNB. Industry standard for classical text classification pipelines.
AutoModelForSequenceClassification, Trainer, and pipeline("text-classification"). The go-to for fine-tuning transformer classifiers.

Few-shot learning framework. Fine-tunes a sentence transformer with contrastive learning on 8–64 labeled examples and then trains a logistic regression head. Often outperforms full fine-tuning at < 100 examples.
Annotation tools for building labeled datasets. Prodigy includes active learning workflows to prioritise the most informative examples for labeling.

Spammers craft messages that evade classifiers by substituting characters (e.g., "v1agra" instead of "viagra") or inserting invisible Unicode characters. Use character-level features or normalise inputs before classification.

Text patterns drift over time (new slang, new product names). Classification accuracy degrades silently without monitoring. Set up accuracy monitoring on a sample of human-reviewed predictions monthly.

A sentiment classifier trained on English product reviews may misclassify sentiment in non-English code-switched text. Audit model performance separately by language, demographic, and domain before production deployment.
Text classification maps a string to a label from a fixed set; variants include binary, multiclass, multi-label, and ordinal classification.
Always establish a TF-IDF + logistic regression baseline before investing in transformer fine-tuning — it is often within a few percentage points of BERT at 100× lower compute cost.
Use zero-shot classification (NLI-based) when you have no labeled data at all; use SetFit when you have fewer than 100 labeled examples.
Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets; prefer macro-F1, per-class F1, and PR-AUC.
Set a confidence threshold and route low-confidence predictions to human review — automation with an escape hatch is safer than fully automated classification.
Fit the TF-IDF vectoriser only on training data to avoid label leakage through the vocabulary.
Monitor class distribution and confidence scores in production to detect distribution shift before accuracy degrades.
